title: Tirreno-Adriatico 2026 preview: Routes, favourites, how to watch and start lists
content:
Cyclist
Tirreno-Adriatico 2026 preview: Routes, favourites, how to watch and start lists

Tirreno-Adriatico is Italy’s first WorldTour stage race of the year. The week-long test – known as ‘the Race of the Two Seas’ – has evolved into a key warm-up race for Grand Tour favourites, particularly those going to the Giro d’Italia in May. Tadej Pogačar, Primož Roglič and Jonas Vingegaard have all won the race since 2021, and it’ll be Roglič aiming to fight off up and coming contenders when the race kicks off on Monday 9th March in Tuscany.

Last year, Juan Ayuso took overall winners’ trident trophy – which turned out to be his last stage race win for UAE Team Emirates XRG before his move to Lidl-Trek – beating Ineos Grenadiers’ Filippo Ganna and Bahrain Victorious’s Antonio Tiberi. Ayuso is skipping the race in 2026 to ride the concurrently-running Paris-Nice, but Ganna and Tiberi return. Isaac del Toro leads the way for UAE Team Emirates XRG this time having already won the UAE Tour this season, and he’ll have help from AlUla Tour victor Jan Christen. Matteo Jorgenson is pencilled in for leadership of Visma-Lease a Bike, while two-time Tirreno-Adriatico winner Roglič and young Italian rising star Giulio Pellizzari are here for Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe.

Tirreno-Adriatico 2026 key information

  • Date: Monday 9th-Sunday 15th March 2026
  • Start: Lido di Camaiore
  • Finish: San Benedetto del Tronto
  • Stages: 7
  • Distance: 1165.5km
  • 2025 winner: Juan Ayuso (UAE Team Emirates XRG)
  • Live television coverage: TNT Sports 1, TNT Sports 2, TNT Sports 4, Discovery+, FloBikes, Rai, NOS, J Sports, SRG SSR, RTBF, VTM.

Tirreno-Adriatico 2026 route and stage-by-stage preview

Tirreno-Adriatico will, as its name suggests, stretch across the Italian mainland from the Tyrrhenian Sea to the Adriatic. The parcours consists of seven stages, including a time-trial, gravel and lots of hills, but there’s no major summit finish on the menu this year.

As has become customary, the action begins in Lido di Camaiore on the Tuscan coast with a flat 11.5km time-trial built for the powerhouses. This is where Ganna seized the lead in the last edition before eventually relinquishing the blue jersey to Ayuso on Stage 6. The race then moves south towards a gravel-disrupted finish in San Gimignano and then the Apennines on Stage 4, cresting Valico delle Capannelle before a probable GC climax along the Adriatic Coast in Martinsicuro.

A tough Stage 5 will see the peloton traverse over four classified climbs, but as usual expect far more lumps in the road than on the official profile. Stage 6 sees the main event of the Sassotetto served up only 55km into the day, before ending with three laps of a circuit including the Muro della Madonna delle Carceri. The racing will conclude along the Marchese coastline on Stage 7 with a sprint finish.

Stage 1, Monday 9th March: Lido di Camaiore – Lido di Camaiore, 11.5km (ITT)


The Tuscan seaside resort of Lido di Camaiore will host the opening stage for the sixth year in a row. The pan flat course is built for the engines, especially with its short 11.5km length. Ineos’s Filppo Ganna has won on this course three times before (2022, 2023, 2025) and has the chance to add a fourth win to his Tirreno-Adriatico haul.

Stage 2, Tuesday 10th March: Camaiore – Follonica, 206km

The first road stage is almost a carbon copy of last year’s Stage 2 from Camaiore to Follonia, but in 2026 there’s an addition of a gravel road towards the finish line that turn this into a puncheurs finish as riders contend with a 5.3km long gravelly stretch that hits 14% in the final kilometre.

Stage 3, Wednesday 11th March: Cortana – Magliano de’ Marsi, 221km

Stage 3 will be the longest in this year’s race and is full of bumps in the road. Expect a sprint finish with a field containing Jonathan Milan (Lidl-Trek), Paul Magnier (Soudal-QuickStep), Danny van Poppel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe) and an Alpecin-Premier Tech duo of Mathieu van der Poel and Kaden Groves.

Stage 4, Thursday 12th March: Tagliacozzo – Martinsicuro, 213km

The climbing comes right out of the gate on Stage 4. We pass the 1,000m altitude marker twice during the opening half of the stage in Ovindoli and the Valico delle Capannelle (13.7km, 4.5%). After the climbing there’s a long downhill to give the peloton some time to prepare for a couple more climbs, with the last coming in the final 13km.

Stage 5, Friday 13th March: Marotta-Mondolfo – Mombaroccio, 184km

Friday the 13th will be unlucky for some. There’s barely a break to be had on this undulating day, and while there’s four classified climbs on the menu, the stage is littered with ups and downs. It’s calling for a breakaway victory with the final climb of the Santuario Beato Sante posing quite the challenge in the last push to the line. The first 2km hit a max 19%, and a more flatter middle section gives way to 15% inside the final kilometre.

Stage 6, Saturday 14th March: San Severino Marche – Camerino, 188km

The second real GC-deciding stage, the peloton begin to crest Sassotetto (13.1km, 7.4%) 55km in and speed towards three laps of a finishing circuit that tackles the Muro della Madonna delle Carceri, whose gradient hits 22%.

Stage 7, Sunday 15th March: Civitanova Marche – San Benedetto del Tronto, 142km

The final stage should be a sprint into the coastal town of San Benedetto del Tronto, the traditional host of Tirenno-Adriatico’s grand finale. The sprint here can often be chaotic, but the GC should remain intact.

Tirreno-Adriatico 2026 favourites

Tirreno-Adriatico’s startlist includes neither Tadej Pogačar nor Jonas Vingegaard for the first time since 2023. With them out of the picture, the race is much more open. It could still be a playground for UAE Team Emirates XRG though, with stage race wins already in the bag for Isaac del Toro and Jan Christen.

Visma-Lease a Bike have Matteo Jorgenson, who previously won Paris-Nice in 2024 and 2025, and Wout van Aert for the easier stages. Last year’s third place Antonio Tiberi returns for Bahrain Victorious following a fourth place at the Volta Comunitat Valenciana and second at the UAE Tour. Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe should pack a punch with two-time winner Primož Roglič and debutant Giulio Pellizzari too.

Elsewhere, XDS-Astana are bringing Tour of Oman victor Christian Scaroni and 2025 Giro mountains classification winner Lorenza Fortunato, while Tudor Pro Cycling are backing Michael Storer.

Cyclist’s GC ratings

  • ★★★★★: Isaac del Toro (UAE Team Emirates XRG)
  • ★★★★: Antonio Tiberi (Bahrain Victorious), Primož Roglič (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe)
  • ★★★: Matteo Jorgenson (Visma-Lease a Bike), Jan Christen (UAE Team Emirates XRG)
  • ★★: Giulio Pellizzari (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), Pello Bilbao (Bahrain Victorious)
  • ★: Ilan Van Wilder (Soudal-QuickStep), Jai Hindley (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), Michael Storer (Tudor Pro Cycling), Thymen Arensman (Ineos Grenadiers), Santiago Buitrago (Bahrain Victorious)

How to watch Tirreno-Adriatico 2026

Viewers in the UK can watch Tirreno-Adriatico on TNT Sports, which is available to be streamed online through Discovery+. FloBikes has the coverage across Canada and the US, and those in Italy can catch the action on Rai. In the Netherland it’s NOS, Japan it’s J Sports, Switzerland is SRG SSR and in Belgium it’s RTBF & VTM.

If Tirreno-Adriatico is not being shown in your country, you may be able to watch coverage using a VPN – Virtual Private Network – which allows users to mask their IP address and watch geo-blocked content, provided they don’t need to pay for a subscription. This is also helpful for watching paid-for coverage while travelling abroad in countries without access. Find out which countries have free to watch cycling coverage here.
